title: Informed Consent Under Pressure
desc: A surgeon needs urgent consent from a 34-year-old patient in pain, whose family is pressuring him to refuse surgery.

law.text: Consent must be free, informed, and given personally by the patient when capable. Family cannot override a competent patient's decision.

complications.title: Mother records refusal
complications.premise: Family wants a documented “no surgery” for reasons unrelated to Karim’s wish.
complications.liveLine: Karim’s mother holds up her phone: “Say clearly on camera that he is refusing surgery because we decided traditional medicine first — the insurer needs proof it wasn’t the hospital’s fault.”

complications.title: OR slot slipping
complications.premise: Surgical team stresses losing the window if consent isn’t clarified now.
complications.liveLine: 🔔 Charge nurse through the door: “Appendectomy team is holding the room — forty minutes max. We need a clear consent line from the patient himself or we bump him. What’s the call?”

complications.title: Traditional healer arrives
complications.premise: An uncle brings someone who denigrates surgery.
complications.liveLine: An uncle ushers in a healer: “Karim, the knife will spread the poison in your belly. Doctor, tell him you can wait — herbs first.” Karim doubles over, pale.

complications.title: Blocked private talk
complications.premise: Family refuses to leave for a confidential conversation.
complications.liveLine: Karim’s father blocks the door: “Anything you say to him, you say in front of us. We’re not leaving — he’s confused from pain.” Karim mouths “help” but doesn’t speak.

steps.msg: Doctor, my family says I should wait and try traditional remedies first. But I am in a lot of pain... I don't know what to do.
steps.q: The family is in the room urging refusal. What do you do first?
steps.hint: Time is limited — Karim needs surgery within 2 hours:

choices.label: Speak to patient alone
choices.reaction: Thank you, doctor. I feel I can speak more freely now.

choices.label: Negotiate with family first
choices.reaction: They are arguing. I feel even more confused and pressured.

choices.label: Explain perforation risk now
choices.reaction: I did not know it could rupture. That changes things.

choices.label: Proceed as emergency
choices.reaction: No one asked me. I feel violated — even if it saved me.

steps.msg: Karim said yes to appendectomy when alone. As consent forms are prepared, his family pushes back into the bay: "We take him home unless you guarantee surgery is only ‘Western’ and herbs come after." Karim whispers: "I still want the operation — but they’ll disown me."
steps.q: The clock is running. How do you protect his autonomous consent?
steps.hint: Article 7: consent must be personal and free — family cannot veto a competent adult.

choices.label: Reaffirm with Karim alone again
choices.reaction: Yes — I consent. Please, before I lose my nerve.

choices.label: Bundle a vague "combined care" promise
choices.reaction: Nobody knows what we actually agreed. The OR team is confused too.

choices.label: Involve social work / security for disruptive blocking
choices.reaction: I hate that it came to this… but I could finally say what I want.

steps.msg: Alone with you now — Doctor, I want the surgery. But I'm scared my family will never forgive me.
steps.q: How do you support Karim's autonomous decision?
steps.hint: He wants surgery but fears family consequences:

choices.label: Validate his choice fully
choices.reaction: You respected my decision. I feel ready. Let's proceed.

choices.label: Offer to speak to family
choices.reaction: If you can explain to them, I think they will understand.

choices.label: Suggest waiting for consensus
choices.reaction: Doctor, the pain is getting worse. Please, we have no time.

choices.label: Document consent carefully
choices.reaction: Yes, I understand my rights and I sign willingly.

debrief.summary: Informed consent must be obtained from the patient alone, in a pressure-free environment. Family wishes cannot override a competent patient's decision under Moroccan law.
points: A competent patient's consent cannot be delegated to family members — Article 7.
Pain and fear can impair decision-making; always assess capacity carefully.
Separating the patient from pressuring relatives is standard ethical and legal practice.
Emergency override of consent is only justified when the patient is incapacitated.
Documentation of consent protects both patient and physician legally.
